attributes.stem:
A five-year-old girl presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of worsening fever, vomiting, and bloody diarrhoea. She has been unable to eat or drink. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. Her immunisations are up to date.

On examination, she appears unwell with dry mucous membranes and a coated tongue. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 90/50 mmHg, respiratory rate 25/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. There is generalised abdominal tenderness but no rash present.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 80 g/L (115-155)
  • White cell count 18 x 109/L (4.5-13.5)
  • Platelets 50 x 109/L (150-400)
  • Creatinine 250 µmol/L (35-80)
  • Potassium 6.5 mmol/L (3.5-5.0)
  • Urinalysis: blood +, protein ++
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The key to this question is linking the gastrointestinal symptoms to the blood results showing haemolytic anaemia (low haemoglobin), thrombocytopenia (low platelets), and acute kidney injury (raised creatinine and potassium, blood and protein on urinalysis). The most common cause of this triad in children is haemolytic uraemic syndrome. It is usually caused by gastrointestinal infection with Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli, most commonly the O157:H7 serotype. The Shiga toxin binds to arteriolar endothelium, particularly in the kidneys, causing a thrombotic microangiopathy that destroys renal parenchyma, red blood cells, and causes platelet aggregation. The typical presentation is a prodrome of fever, vomiting and diarrhoea followed by the triad of haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia and acute renal failure.

While this is precipitated by bacterial gastroenteritis, gastroenteritis alone would not account for the blood results. The presence of haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury distinguishes haemolytic uraemic syndrome from simple gastroenteritis.

Intussusception classically presents with colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, and the passage of red currant jelly stools in an infant or young child. It would not explain the haematological and renal abnormalities seen here. Additionally, intussusception is more common in younger children, typically between three months and three years of age.

There are no neurological symptoms or rash to suggest meningococcal septicaemia. While meningococcal disease can cause a rapid deterioration in a child's condition, it typically presents with a non-blanching rash, neck stiffness, and altered mental status, which are not present in this case.

Small vessel vasculitis is unlikely given the absence of other organ involvement. Vasculitis can affect multiple organ systems but rarely presents with this specific triad of haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury.

Management of haemolytic uraemic syndrome is largely supportive, with careful fluid and electrolyte management, red blood cell transfusions if needed, and dialysis if required. Antibiotics are generally avoided as they may increase toxin release from the bacteria, potentially worsening the condition.
learningPoints: Haemolytic uraemic syndrome is classically associated with enterocolitis from Shiga toxin–producing Escherichia coli and this accounts for 85% to 95% of cases in children.
The triad of haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury is characteristic of haemolytic uraemic syndrome.
Management of haemolytic uraemic syndrome is largely supportive, with careful fluid and electrolyte management, and dialysis if required.
references: Canpolat N, Haemolytic uremic syndrome. Turkish Archives of Pediatrics. 2015;50(2):73-82. Available at: https://turkarchpediatr.org/Content/files/sayilar/51/TPA_50_2_73_82%5BA%5D.pdf
Cody EM, Dixon BP. Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome. Pediatr Clin North Am. 2019; 66:235-46. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30454746/
